relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jacob lifted his feet and moved eastward into the next shaping of his life.
He saw a well in the field, stone-covered and waiting for gathered flocks.
Water was opened only when the flocks gathered and the stone was rolled away.
Jacob asked the shepherds where they were from, and Haran appeared.
He asked for Laban, and the family thread was found.
Peace was reported, and Rachel came into view with the sheep.
Jacob urged the shepherds to water and pasture the flock.
They waited for the gathered moment and the rolling of the stone.
Rachel came as shepherdess while Jacob was still speaking.
Seeing Rachel, Jacob rolled away the stone and watered Laban’s sheep.
Jacob kissed Rachel, lifted his voice, and wept.
He told her he was Rebekah’s son, and she ran to tell her father.
Laban ran to meet Jacob and brought him into the house.
Kinship was named: bone and flesh.
Laban turned kinship into wages and service.
Two daughters stood before the story: Leah the older, Rachel the younger.
Leah’s tender eyes and Rachel’s beauty are placed side by side.
Jacob loved Rachel and offered seven years of service for her.
Laban accepted the arrangement.
Seven years became like a few days because of Jacob’s love for Rachel.
Jacob asked for the marriage his completed days had earned.
Laban gathered the men and made a feast.
In the night, Laban brought Leah instead of Rachel.
Zilpah was given into Leah’s household.
Morning revealed Leah, and Jacob the deceiver found himself deceived.
Laban answered with the law of firstborn before younger, reflecting Jacob’s own wound back to him.
Jacob was bound into another seven years for Rachel.
He completed the week and received Rachel too.
Bilhah was given into Rachel’s household.
Jacob loved Rachel more than Leah and served seven more years.
The Eternal saw Leah’s unloved place and opened her womb.
Reuben was born from Leah’s longing to be seen and loved.
Simeon was born from Leah’s cry to be heard.
Levi was born from Leah’s hope to be joined.
Judah was born when Leah turned from longing for Jacob toward praise of the Eternal.
